content: My opinion of the app has been dropping a lot lately. Removing the sentence discussions has been a huge loss. I made heavy use of those to understand mistakes or sentences I didn't quite get. Now, many mistakes leave you confused and with no explanation or way to figure out what you did wrong. Overall, Duolingo is not a bad app for supplementing language learning. However, it falters as a primary source and it seems to be getting worse and not better. Additionally, Latin support is a joke.

content: The new energy system is terrible. Before, you were only penalized if you answered incorrectly. Now, both correct and incorrect answers deduct from your energy (which is capped at 25) and the only way to earn more is to purchase it or watch an ad for 5 energy. Energy also always conveniently runs out mid lessons. And lately, when I watch ads to earn more energy, the entire ad plays and I don't ever get awarded my energy.

content: While it started good, this app has quickly spiraled into something I dread doing. For starters, every single time challenge is far too short and should be at least 2 to 5 minutes longer. Especially in the beginning stages. Second, I have trouble spelling, I always have and always will. But lately 100% of the tests require spelling so I'm spending 10 minutes just trying to spell one word, and I'm sorry, but when you fail repeatedly, it doesn't make you want to continue. I dislike this app.

content: There's an ongoing increase of strange or inaccurate translations, and the use of AI is being pushed heavily. I primarily use it for review of Japanese (I've been learning from various sources for years), but I feel uneasy using it for Spanish, because I won't be able to detect those errors because I'm still a beginner in that language. Previously, I felt like Duolingo was a good way to start learning, but now I think using a well made Anki deck combined with a textbook would be the way to go.

content: I wish I could give zero stars. Duo was actually a great app when it started. Now, the best features are gone. Be very careful. Their free trial is extremely predatory. The page will force you to accept a "gift" of "free days," but that will activate the free trial. Once the surprise free trial is over, it will auto-sign you up for the entire year, the most expensive option, without your approval. I was charged over $100 and they will not refund. Same thing happened to 3 family members.

content: The app is great for learning languages, and it drills the words in your brain. Although it is annoying to run out of hearts, the app is extremely helpful. You can choose between beginner and intermediate levels, which is great, and the leaderboard motivates you to do more. Also, Duolingo does not have any ads unless you choose one to get a heart. This helps you focus better on the language you are learning. It allows you to reveiw skills in case you forgot and that alone is very helpful.

content: The app was useful and fun to use for learning. I dropped my rating down to 1 star because they changed how the teaching is accessed. It used to be that you had hearts and lost one for every wrong answer, but now they have an energy system, where every question costs energy. It is obviously a ploy to force people to buy their premium packages, which is going to make a lot of free users, like me, go and find a better app. I do not recommend this app.

content: Fun, gamified language learning, but I would say it's a great "starter" app for learning a new language, not if you want to develop deep understanding or expertise in a new language. It's good for learning vocabulary. After talking with several of my Spanish speaking friends, it's not always accurate, and the example phrases are sometimes bizarre, but I get why they do the silly phrases. Overall, it's been a fantastic family learning tool for my wife and kids.

content: Purchased an annual subscription for this and regretting. New higher tier subscription came out since and I'm constantly nagged to updated. The whole trophy system is just silly and greatly flawed. Lessons don't scale in difficulty linearly. It goes from 0-60 quite quickly. Duolingo definitely helped at first, but I didn't need the premium version to get there, and now it's lessons are just too difficult and there is no bridge. I'll definitely be switching to another app to continue learning.

content: There are some serious holes in the learning. The voice speaks quickly in the other language and there are no pronunciation guides to show you how letters, groups of letters, or words are pronounced so have no real idea if you're pronouncing anything correctly. After not missing a day, mostly twice a day, for 4.7 years, I still have no ability to hold even a basic conversation. Also, if you force stop the app, it will just turn itself back on, which is really bad.

content: It has come a long way over the years. Very interactive. That would get five stars, but the whole energy system is super wacky.. It's like the whole app is designed to kick you out ASAP, you lose all of your energy in 1-2 lessons (with combos..), so you're done with the app for the day in ~5 min. Or less if you're just reviewing material, in which case that's all you're doing for the day. A lot of ads too, but that doesn't really bother me.

content: Useful, efficient, easy to learn from. However I'm recently running into a couple problems; Sometimes the voice option bugs out and keeps saying it's wrong even when you're very clearly saying the word. The second is that with the timed star challenges, sometimes you get near the end without even running out of time and then it'll just stop and say something about you getting XP but the star isn't given. Overall I love the app, just thought I'd bring up those couple issues. :)

content: VERY FRUSTRATING! Each lesson begins by throwing you into the deep end - new words appear without any notice or translation, forcing the student to guess at its meaning. Same goes for translating the writing - most times you have to keep guessing, and if you get it wrong, the lesson makes you do it over and over until you get it right. If this were an in-person college course, I would drop it!

content: My husband and I have been using Duolingo to practice Spanish for almost 2 years.We pay for a subscription. We are strongly considering quitting. It really is just endless quizzes at this point. The material is rarely covered in any other way. Just endless quizzes and tests. If you need help, click on a word to get some help and input the word exactly as you see it in a sentence. then it will tell you that that was wrong and the correct word wasn't one of the options. It's incredibly frustrating

content: It's fantastic until you get to conjugating verbs... which it doesn't show or explain how/why/when to do AT ALL. Vocabulary section also doesn't show the various forms of the verbs it teaches you either. I ended up just writing them out and making my own little study sheet. One other major criticism is that you can't just pay for "Super" on a monthly basis, instead you pay for a lump-sum of time all at once. Overall, though, this app has been making learning Spanish a lot of fun.

content: Overall, I do think it's great for learning a language and I find it helpful most days. However, the fact that now every time you want to earn the three times for the next day you have to finish a unit is impossible. There's definitely a lot of bugs and the energy thing instead of the hearts really makes it less fun. I wish they would go back to how it was about a year ago so that it was easier to use and more motivating. I do like the options in the practice hub now though.

content: I would rate 4.5, but I can't so I rounded it up to five stars! Now, I love Duolingo. It has been helpful to my language learning, and I like the variety of languages it has. I love the simple characters and their designs, especially Zaris! My only criticisms is the fact some less, but not unknown languages (such as nepali) aren't on there, some ads are completely unskippable, and I find the excessive use of AI a bit unnecessary. Overall, one of the best free apps for language learning though!

content: Duolingo gamifies learning new languages if that's your thing, and it does it to the extreme. You won't be taught grammar, but you'll eventually absorb it. You'll be told when you're wrong, but never really told why. Also, the animated characters have nothing to do with the sentences you're translating. There are occasionally tiles where you match up a word with a graphic, but they seem to be rare.

content: Easily the best language learning app. I had this app a while ago and was recommended by my friend to download it again, and it's even better than I remember it! You can learn all the basic languages AND they added math, chess, and music! You can also add your friends on there and do friend quests together! It's so fun with widgets, animations and notifications so you never miss your streak. It's also super fun to do - learning is like a game so you want to play!! I really recommend this app!

content: This app gamifies learning Spanish, which can be fun, but it doesn't really teach you any material. It is straight memorization. Many time, they will ask you the translation of names of people. This does not help you learn at all, you are just filling in the blank with what is given already. There are many instances of this. The free version uses all of the tactics that free to play games use. It is a constant bombardment of ads and trying to get you to join the paid tier. Near worthless app.

content: There is a major problem with this app that is sure to make anybody's motivation to learn tank. When you answer a certain amount of questions, you're supposed to receive extra energy, but if you "run out" toward the end of the lesson, but still receive extra energy, the app will act like the energy isn't there and jeopardize your entire lesson. This is extremely counterintuitive and hindering to people trying to learn new languages.

content: Okay for lower learners. If you wish to learn a language in depth do not count on this app. Grammatical rules will be lost. It had essentially turned into that commercial from the 90's for the language learning video series. They teach you phrases and memorization but not how to apply it fully. Plus, the "team" often reorganizes what they believe is a good learning environment. So after considerable time, they update, and my next lessons include words I've never seen!

content: 3/2026: not what it used to be. Incessant ads. Starting on day 2, when you run out XPs (AKA time, about 12 minutes) you're forced to quit for the day, install extra apps for XPs, or buy a full membership. This app is more a game to spend money or be suckered into advertiser's hands than an educational tool. 6/2025: Updates every few days, 3+ times per week is excessive for the quality of this product. Was better in prior years.

content: It is a reletively fun app, but ultimately fails to teach the most important things of any given language, basic grammar and the structure of the sentence, since a lot of languages move things like verbs around. Or in the case of Japanese has the structure completely wrong in almost every sense, I tried using duolingo as a companion to Japanese in college and had to stop because it was so wrong that my teacher had to correct me every day. I absolutely do not recommend the Japanese course.

content: The energy system saved me. It wasn't worth the effort of using the app to go through two lessons a day before the energy ran out. It made me look at other apps and language learning systems. Today, after 7 years of using the app over multiple languages I'm deleting it. Rather than improving the learning system and gaining users as a result, they changed the profit system and hoped users would come along for the ride. The paid version does not add enough value to justify the cost.

content: Not the best way to learn a language from scratch, especially languages that aren't European (From English at least) but definitely a good way of practicing vocabulary or memorizing characters. I took a Japanese class years ago, and used Duolingo mostly to fortify my knowledge of the characters, and it was extremely helpful, though in the regular lessons I did notice some errors that stuck out like a sore thumb. This app definitely relies on AI, but for a free app is pretty good to start off.

content: This used to be a 5-star review for an app that gamified language learning - hearing people pronounce the words I was learning was very helpful, and I'd always listen close to the nuances in different characters' pronunciation to understand that there's more than one "right way." Now when I listen, I hear the uncanny "je ne sais quoi" of AI-generated voices. I wasn't going to get Max and, at this rate, paying for Super would feel like an endorsement of removing the human element from Duolingo.

content: Look, I love a free app, but this one is very poorly made. The flashcards are straight up broken. What's the Spanish word for "my"? content: For Spanish, it is great. You're not going to become fluent with Duolingo, but it guides your study in a logical order so that you know what subjects to look for material on to help you along where Duo falls short. For German, it is absolute garbage. I was shocked at how poorly put together, buggy, and poorly constructed the German course is after completing the Spanish course. Night and day. I'd say if you are using it to learn Spanish or maybe French, you are OK. Nothing else.

content: I previously rated duolingo 5 stars, but after recent AI changes, it's almost unusable. If you speak at a normal rate, it counts >50% of correct pronunciation as errors. Don't waste your money paying for it. Use the free version. If you insist on more full service, go elsewhere. Amusingly, one of the words it has the hardest time understanding is Duo.

content: It's the perfect app to start learning many different languages. Stick with it for a year, (try to do a unit every day) and you'll 100% understand another language enough to listen to it and have basic responses. The "legendary" and "daily refresh" modes after completing the course is especially useful for getting the most out of Duolingo. Don't get stuck trying to learn every section perfectly. Just keep pushing forward. There's a lot of value here.

content: REMOVE ENERGY!! The energy does not help users who have mistakes as advertised, it just limits how many lessons one can take regardless of performance. content: Love Duolingo! Learning with friends is fun. Leaderboards motivate me. But, Duolingo provides interactive lessons, and lacks grammar rules explanations. It is difficult to grasp the grammar rules with just a lot of examples. It would be useful if you include a phrasebook, word list, more free streak freezes (because of our annual cultural festival where we don't read, write, or study for 2 days) I use it as supplement for my learning along with other 3 apps, so these aren't deal-breakers for me.

content: I give 4.5 but overall, I think full 5 works better than a 4. This is because: PROS: - Fun, making it quick, easy, and enjoyable to learn - Flexible, giving room to grow at almost every level - Full of choices, like if you want to skip ahead, or pick a language to learn CONS: - It can get a little slow or fast, especially for younger children - Sometimes it skips things or the order it teaches it in dosen't make sense Though me and my family still don't regret getting duolingo.

content: I'm actually a little surprised with how popular this app is. I'm gonna guess it's because of Duolingo's presence on social media, because honestly, this isn't that good. It uses AI for it's lessons, which sometimes results in sentences that aren't even grammatically correct. Overall, I think this would be great for learning to READ a language, not speak it, since it doesn't do a good job at teaching accents (which are in almost every language).

content: I like the app. I have learned a lot. The grammar rules for languages need to be clear. Sometimes I don't understand the sentences that I'm putting together. content: I have a 35-day streak and will be deleting it. For one, the app doesn't really teach you anything. There is no explanation of sentence structures. In languages very different from your native one it is pretty necessary to understand how to construct sentences. Also, I don't trust AI to teach me a language properly, quality over quantity. The new direction of laying off employees to have AI software churn out slop just isn't where I want my language learning journey to go. Other apps do better.

content: Duolingo is a good choice for language learning, offering practice conversations and grammar rules, learning is very easy. The few free for a day super trials are considerate, and the way it's designed is very user friendly. However, there are some few issues I run into. For the music courses get very laggy, and feel almost impossible sometimes to hit notes on time. Along with slow loads for Profiles and the News tab. Overall, this is a good choice if wanting to learn a new language for free.

content: AI is making this app a lot worse. The new AI stories are boring and the AI-generated lessons are just padding that adds little and slows progress. There's also a lot of weird bugs, like only accepting the English pronunciation for Euro in the German speaking exercises. All of this is on top of an app that doesn't really teach any language very well (and some languages are embarrassingly bad). Duolingo is okay for learning some basics while using the toilet, but not very good for much else.

content: I used to really love this app, however, it has progressively got worse. Learning has become too heavily gamified, and grammer is difficult to reiview without paying for a subscription to access templates or practice sessions. The 'hearts' system has also become a pain. If stuck on a difficult section, one has to repeatly alternate between a practice session that has no basis is current learning and the main event. There are better apps for each specific language, that I reccomend people look to

content: The French course has a lot of excellent and wonderfully humorous content, but I'm noticing too many errors and inconsistencies. For the price, the material should be very well edited, by humans. The AI voices mispronounce some words—unacceptable in a language course! Dialogue transcripts were available for a while, but no longer. Fancy new features are nice, but proofreading the content should take priority. The "Match Madness" activity wastes time that could be spent actually learning.

content: I have been using Duolingo on and off for years. I've been currently using it to learn Japanese for about 9 months, and I'm really liking it! Of course, you need to do it alongside some other resource (like Anki for example), but it really helped me learning hiragana and katakana, and it's a great way to force me to review things every day. I don't think I would have kept my streak without the gamification. I love the new features they are introducing (like listening practice in podcast form)!

content: Fine for hobbyists & building habits in a fun way, but not for efficient language learning. XP is a meaningless indicator of progress and fluency. There's no way to set desired proficiency by some date, you can't practice speaking with prompts on your own, and they don't have real convos you can listen to & translate. (Busuu does great with this.) Premium is also not worth it. I'd look elsewhere for real language learning, or expect to supplement Duolingo with other learning.

content: Less useful than it used to be, but still the best free language learning app I know of. They've deliberately removed a couple of features (like practice when you'd run out of hearts for lessons and explanations if you get a question wrong) that greatly diminish the true learning potential of this app for free players. It's hard to truly learn if you don't get regular feedback on your mistakes and practice. If you're struggling, stars can disappear in one bad lesson. Very disappointing.

Sometimes I wish I'd not renewed my subscription.

content: Each lesson was based on pitch and rhythm - A, B, C; quarter, half, whole, etc. - and you'd practice those before doing a song. content: This app has undergone many changes in just the past couple of years. Once, it contained lessons, lectures, explanations of grammatical and syntactical structures, and comment threads for learners to "study together," to engage with each other to offer a hand where needed. It used to offer techniques to guide users away from making mistakes. The developers have removed those features, as well as the opportunity to practice. If you can't practice, you can't learn; I advise choosing another app.

I don't think I could stand it without.

content: I've used duolingo for several years and have a 852 score streak. I enjoy using it, it's helped immerse me in my Greek heritage. But I don't think it's an effective teacher. The app emphasizes competition and simplicity over effective teaching. I didn't touch numbers in Greek until a year in, which is insane. As of 27/11/24 practice has been greyed out for free users (a free source of hearts) and xp reduced which is very greedy. I'll continue using, but there are legitimate issues.

content: The quality of the translations has drastically dropped since the introduction of AI. content: After using this app for ~2 years, I will break my +670 days streak. The intrusive ads (you no longer practice to get hearts, you see ads), the high prices and the AI-first update made this app worse, so learning here isn't fun anymore. I recommend better apps like Busuu, Memrise, Babbel or Lingodeer

content: Duolingo’s German course is a letdown for someone with prior language experience. After studying German from 6th to 12th grade, I hoped to refresh my skills, but the app’s AI-driven lessons lack the nuance of human expertise. Many correct answers are marked wrong, ignoring cultural and linguistic subtleties. It’s clear Duolingo prioritizes AI over quality, resulting in inaccurate lessons. I won’t pay for a subscription to a lazy, error-filled app. 1/5 stars for poor accuracy and reliance on AI.

content: Duolingo’s simple design makes language learning fun and easy to follow. The gamified lessons are great for beginners, but the hearts mechanic is a major drawback. Running out of hearts after mistakes slows progress, and while Duolingo Plus removes this, it’s not worth the investment without grammar. The app focuses on trial and error without teaching grammar rules, making learning less effective. Duolingo would greatly improve by explaining grammar concepts instead of relying on repetition.

content: I'm a linguist, I used to find the app amusing and it's a great way to kill time and sort of learn something. I have a 1600 day steak, I've been a user since the start of it. As I was between plans, I got a glimpse at the free version, you get an ad with every lesson unless you pay. This last change made me hate the app and I don't care about the streak now.

content: The new energy system ruined the app and significantly hinders progress. The (Japanese) lessons are OK, with a good variety of useful vocabulary. However, the limited energy means you can scarcely finish more than two short lessons per day without paying, even if you make no mistakes. It seems like a real stretch to claim that this is a "free" language learning app any more. Please bring back the old hearts system.

content: I have two complaints about Duolingo. Firstly, it's heavily modelled around the premium - Super Duolingo. If you want to do more than about 3 lessons per day, you have to buy more energy or pay for super. For the leagues, it's impossible to do very well without super. Secondly, it teaches you zero grammar, expecting you to pick it up as you go. You might be able to listen to others, but you will struggle to speak it yourself without using it as a supplement. Chess course is decent, so 2 stars.

content: I wanted to learn Swahili. Not many such apps do that. So all credit to Duolingo for including it. Thank you! I'm enjoying it and learning, but there's a few niggles that go with a less popular course. - The teaching method is lots of exposure - good, but it's a bit confusing to be told you're wrong when the English (or the Swahili) is ambiguous e.g "you read a book" - singular or plural, present or past? - The example sentence bank is limited, so patterns are less clear. Hence 4* not 5*.

content: This app is extremely useful and truly number one. However, there is one issue that feels quite serious to me. The available language courses stop at lower levels, which limits progress. For example, in English I can only study up to B2, but there are no options for C1 or C2. For German, the situation is even worse: the courses end around high B1, with no continuation into B2, C1, or C2. The same problem exists for Spanish, Italian, and French. I am also a paying customer of Duolingo.

content: After a year of use, data retention for answers is still a problem, but it's now a MAJOR issue since they switched to an energy based system where every answer gets an energy point deduction. Why? Because when their app lags and crashes (which happens 11 out of 14 times I used it in a week), you lose the energy points and it doesn't count towards your goals. The heart system was a better fit for a buggy app since there's no penalty whenever the app crashes. No improvement on lesson quality too.

content: Been doing the Chinese course for almost 2 years. I'd say good for starting/ retaining interest in the language at the very beginning but after that it's definitely not a good primary language learning app. Duolingo will give you a bunch of random words, some are relevant, most are not. It is much closer to a game than to a teaching app. There is not much freedom with what you can learn, and the course dosent teach important grammar/ cultural topics (e.g., Tones, Sentence Structure, etc.).

content: Been using Duo for a year, and I like the base game a lot. But the "Energy" system just ruins it. There's no reward for doing perfect lessons - a perfect score should refill your energy, back to what you started the lesson with. Now you get like +2 and soon you are down to zero anyway. It's so bad, you are forcing people to pay for the app; skill is not rewarded enough. It feels very greedy.

content: For context: I have the super subscription Like with all subscr. based services, the main goal is not to help you achieve your goal ASAP but to keep you hooked for as long as possible while giving you the impression that you're making great progress They regurgitate THE SAME 10-20 words over and over in every lesson. content: I'm learning Korean on Duolingo, and for me, it's a really fun learning app. It's great that you can have friend streaks, you can compete with other learners, you can learn hangul (Korean alphabet) or in general new letters like the Japanese ones, the Chinese ones, you can go back to your mistakes to improve on the things you're not sure about..and a thing that I really like is that you can share phrases with the community. content: Don't rely on this to teach you a language properly. It's all over the place on its approach to grammar, vocab is random & often not useful for day today speaking (it even has the wrong meaning for some vocab) and woefully inconsistent at understanding you. If you have any sort of accent it won't figure you out sometimes even if your Japanese tutor / native speakers understand you perfectly. The gamified approach can be fun sometimes but it's useless at telling you what you need to know.

content: Generally, seeing apps that teach valuable skills easily makes us wonder about the kind of facilities we get, until I slid into this app. This not only taught me what I wanted to learn, but made me believe that these opportunities still exist. content: The change to an energy system makes Duolingo strictly worse than traditional and cheaply accessible language learning tools which are designed by experts. It's now impossible to meaningfully brush up on existing skills, let alone make progress, each day without a subscription. That's OK - any service provider has a right to charge for what they do. But there are vastly better courses for the same or less than a Duolingo subscription, and you can still do a little each day. Do your research!

content: After achieving a streak of 2000 days, I am ready to uninstall this app and never return. Over the past year, the lessons have become repetitive and less creative (I wouldn't be surprised if AI had a hand in that). The ads are increasingly more intrusive and often freeze my app, meaning I have to restart my lessons. Half the features are stuck behind a paywall as well. What was once a fun way to learn new languages is now obviously just a way to squeeze as much money from its users as possible.

content: teaches you some words and or set phrases, but absolutely ignores the most important part of language learning which is understanding the why. There is almost 0 explanation on grammar points, pronunciation points, etc. For japanese it teaches you a mixture of polite and causal speech and uses it together which is a big no no. For chess, the same thing, it gives you some very basic puzzles but doesn't explain the why, the advantages etc

content: been using the app for nearly 10 years so I like it, but Im recently learning Chinese and despise the fact that I have to learn 'hanzi' (chinese writing). my goal is to be conversational in the language, I don't need to learn the WHOLE language. sometimes I just can't answer the question because I can't remember all the details of the Chinese characters and this sucks. please Duolingo, let there be an option to turn specific types of lessons off so that people WANT to continue using your app!

content: been using this for over a year now with the paid subscription (without Max). it's pretty good for picking up basics of new languages. but there are some glitches that skewer the overall experience. the latest one for me in an issue with the double/triple XP rewards for completing daily quests. it's supposed to add up to 30mins plus 15mins by default and another 15 from the night chest. content: this is not an app designed by linguists. Great user interface and general app structure. However, the vocabulary building process is missing, as are key conceptual lesson. Over a month in and vocabulary for a simple conversation is missing and i still can only order iced tea, coffee, soup and porridge. it's not a rapid learning tool. I wouldn't waste your time or money if you're serious about speaking another language, or even if you simply need to learn a few phrases to help with your travels.

content: Was good, bad now. Duolingo used to be fine, sure it was inaccurate sometimes but you could learn a thing or two about languages, now though, even if you make ZERO mistakes, you can only do a few lessons before having to pay because you run out of "energy" and the only way to get around it is by paying. And if you're doing multiple courses, you're energy is shared between them so you can only do a limited amount of courses!

content: it's nice for learning, but it's pretty predatory that they removed the option to practice to regain hearts, so if you fail a lesson you pretty much either have to spend gems or watch ads. (edit: even more predatory now with the introduction of energy instead of hearts. When before you could technically study as much as you wanted for free until you made too many mistakes, now you cant do more than 2 lessons in a row for free, I hate what they are doing to this once amazing app.)

content: I thought the memes were funny, but seriously, Duolingo has taught me faster and more engagingly than any other app I've tried. I used to have 3 for the different parts of Japanese; but Duo has them all in one place, for free. Ads are kinda long, but most are for Plus anyway. There's a competitive league if you like a challenge, and a friend system. So, mostly worth it. Edit 2026: really hate the icon moving from page to page in my phone every time it wants me to practise. No fix so far.
